sections.title: Inversions with Negative Adverbial Words and Phrases

rows.label: hardly / scarcely / barely (... when)
items: Hardly had the flight landed when passengers started reaching for their phones.
Scarcely had the new semester begun when the students were handed their first assignment.
Barely had we stepped off the train when it began to pour with rain.

rows.label: no sooner (... than)
items: No sooner had the concert ended than the audience demanded an encore.

rows.label: only in / only after / only when
items: Only in exceptional circumstances should you access a patient's personal records.
Only after the specialist had confirmed the diagnosis did we begin to grasp the full seriousness.
Only when both sides reach a consensus will the negotiations be considered a success.

rows.label: not until
items: Not until the final results are published will we know who has won the championship.

rows.label: at no time/point/stage
items: At no point did the researcher claim that the findings were conclusive.

rows.label: in no way
items: In no way does this report imply that the company acted dishonestly.

rows.label: little
items: Little did Hannah know that her colleagues had organised a farewell party.

rows.label: never
items: Never have I witnessed such an extraordinary display of skill!

rows.label: not
items: Not a single complaint did the hotel receive throughout the entire season.

rows.label: not only (... but also/too)
items: Not only has the new policy cut waiting times but it has also greatly improved patient satisfaction.

rows.label: on no account
items: On no account should you leave valuables visible inside your vehicle.

rows.label: rarely
items: Rarely does the local council take residents' concerns seriously.

rows.label: seldom
items: Seldom do recent graduates find their ideal job straight after finishing university.

rows.label: under no circumstances
items: Under no circumstances will the event organisers cancel proceedings due to light rain.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: With not until and only (when/after), invert the auxiliary and subject in the main clause.
examples: ✓ Not until the board reviews the evidence will the final decision be announced.
✓ Only when both teams agree on the terms can negotiations continue.

sections.title: Inversions with Adverbial Expressions of Place (+ verb of movement/position)

rows.label: here
items: Here comes the head of department now.

rows.label: there
items: There stood the competition winner, clutching her trophy.

rows.label: adverbial phrases
items: At the heart of the old city stands the ancient cathedral.
Opposite the railway station was a small but welcoming café.
In the basement of the archive building are stored thousands of historical documents.
On the steps of the gallery sat a group of art students, quietly sketching.

rows.label: participle phrases
items: Rushing through the terminal was a businessman with a laptop bag slung over one shoulder.

rows.label: in short answers
items: 'I've already seen that documentary.' 'Have you? So have I.'
'I don't think the new manager is particularly approachable.' 'No, neither do I.'

rows.label: after as, than, so and such
items: I find the new curriculum very demanding, as do most of my colleagues.
Students at this school perform better in the sciences than do pupils at neighbouring schools.
So complex is the immigration process that many applicants give up halfway through.
Such public enthusiasm was there for the new gallery that tickets sold out within hours.

rows.label: in conditional sentences
items: Were the chief executive to step down, it would create uncertainty throughout the whole company.
Should the chief executive step down, it would create uncertainty throughout the whole company.
Had I known about the transport disruptions, I would have left home far earlier.

rows.label: all (that)
items: All that Maya wanted was a little recognition for her hard work.

rows.label: it is/was ... who/which/that
items: It was Daniel who/that alerted the fire brigade.
It was I who submitted the complaint. (formal)
It was me that submitted the complaint. (informal)

rows.label: the ... thing
items: The first thing to do is compile a list of everything that has been reported missing.

rows.label: the day/time/period when/that
items: The summer when the prolonged drought struck was the worst in living memory.

rows.label: the person who/that
items: The person who designed the original suspension bridge was a young engineer from Bristol.

rows.label: the place where
items: The place where the historic signing ceremony took place was a grand eighteenth-century palace.

rows.label: the reason (why)
items: The reason (why) I chose this career was a deep desire to work directly with young people.

rows.label: the thing that
items: The thing that concerns me most is the complete lack of transparency.

rows.label: what
items: What concerns me most is the complete lack of transparency.
What Newton did was revolutionise our entire understanding of motion and gravity.
What happened was that a witness photographed the suspect leaving the building.

rows.label: so
items: Everything moved so quickly that I barely had a chance to take notes.
The queue had been moving so slowly that some people simply gave up and left.
It was so remarkable a performance that the entire audience rose to their feet.
There is so much background noise in this office that concentrating is almost impossible.

rows.label: such
items: The queue had been moving at such a slow pace that some people simply gave up and left.
It was such a remarkable performance that the entire audience rose to their feet.
There is such a lot of background noise in this office that concentrating is almost impossible.

rows.label: too
items: I had too little information available to make a reliable judgement.
The problem appears to be too complex for any straightforward solution.
The ambulance arrived too late to make any real difference to the outcome.

rows.label: enough
items: There simply aren't enough qualified nurses to cover all the shifts.
The candidate wasn't sufficiently confident to convince the interview panel.
She didn't speak loudly enough for the people sitting at the back to hear her.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: So and such can also be used without a that clause. Too is used for a meaning stronger than needed, often with a negative effect. Enough comes before nouns and after adjectives/adverbs.
examples: ✓ There's so much traffic on this road nowadays.
✗ The problem appears to be too complex for any straightforward solution to it.

sections.title: A: Correcting Inversion Errors
sections.instructions: If a phrase in bold is correct, write OK. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Hardly I had sat down at my desk when the phone started ringing.
items.answer: Hardly had I sat down at my desk when the phone started ringing.

items.sentence: Scarcely had the guests arrived when the host announced there had been a change of plan.
We had barely unpacked our suitcases when the hotel fire alarm went off.
items.answer: Barely had we unpacked our suitcases when the hotel fire alarm went off.

items.sentence: No sooner the new safety regulations come into force than businesses find a way around them.
items.answer: No sooner do the new safety regulations come into force than businesses find a way around them.

items.sentence: Scarcely they had presented their findings when the committee began raising objections.
items.answer: Scarcely had they presented their findings when the committee began raising objections.

items.sentence: Hardly the new software had been released when users started reporting serious problems.
items.answer: Hardly had the new software been released when users started reporting serious problems.

items.sentence: It's true that no sooner we had ordered our food than the waiter informed us the kitchen was closing.
items.answer: It's true that no sooner had we ordered our food than the waiter informed us the kitchen was closing.

items.sentence: Barely had the chairperson started to read the report when began the audience to murmur.
items.answer: Barely had the chairperson started to read the report when the audience began to murmur.

items.sentence: Hardly the new road was opened when a serious accident blocked both lanes.
items.answer: Hardly was the new road opened when a serious accident blocked both lanes.

items.sentence: Scarcely Elena had returned from her overseas trip when she was asked to travel again.
items.answer: Scarcely had Elena returned from her overseas trip when she was asked to travel again.

sections.title: B: Completing with Negative Adverbials
sections.instructions: Complete using the words and phrases in the box.

items.sentence: ...... were the technicians to blame for the sudden power outage.
...... once did the new assistant offer to help with the additional workload.
...... completing the mandatory training programme was the applicant offered a permanent post.
...... will spectators be permitted to enter the playing area during a match.
...... were the roads icy, but visibility was also dangerously poor.
...... during the hearing did anyone reveal that the director had already submitted a resignation.
...... did the passengers suspect that the coach driver was an undercover detective.
...... do you come across a musician who combines technical mastery with such genuine emotion.
...... should anyone attempt to access the server without entering a valid password.
...... before has this charity faced such a severe and prolonged shortage of volunteers.

sections.title: C: Completing Inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in bold with the verb in the correct form.

items.sentence: Not until the bridge has been fully inspected ...... (traffic / can pass).
In no way ...... (the report / suggest) that the board of directors was acting irresponsibly.
Seldom ...... (you / encounter) such dedication among first-year undergraduates.
Not only ...... (Sophie / miss) the submission deadline, but she also sent an incomplete draft.
At no time ...... (she / inform) of the proposed changes to the contract.
Only when I had reread the clause ...... (I / understand) exactly what I had agreed to.
Little ...... (Marcus / realise) that his colleagues had already heard about his promotion.
Under no circumstances ...... (you / should share) a client's personal data with any third party.
At no stage ...... (it / seem) likely that the underdog team would actually reach the final.
Rarely ...... (we / see) an organisation as committed to sustainable practices as this one.

sections.title: D: Place Inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using a verb from the box in the correct form.

items.sentence: 'There (1) ...... our new head of department now,' Clara whispered.
I glanced up and walking briskly across the lobby (2) ...... a tall woman in a grey coat.
On her shoulder (3) ...... a leather briefcase, slightly open.
From the conference room above (4) ...... the faint sound of raised voices.
In her other hand (5) ...... a printed copy of the latest quarterly report.
There (6) ...... a young intern, clutching a notepad and looking rather anxious.
On the desk beside him (7) ...... a cold cup of coffee and a pile of unopened correspondence.
Without warning, in the doorway (8) ...... the same woman I had noticed downstairs.

sections.title: E: Choosing Correct Inversions
sections.instructions: Circle the correct phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Paula doesn't really trust the new supplier, and I have to say that neither **I do / do I**.
Such a rare manuscript **it is / is it** that scholars must wear special gloves to handle it.
So **we could / could we** keep an eye on the children, we moved our picnic blanket much closer.
My sister has never tried sushi, and nor **my brother has / has my brother**.
Such **the public interest was / was the public interest** in the trial that extra seating had to be arranged.
It's been a difficult year for the project, as **the previous one was / was the previous one**.
Germany has strict data privacy laws, and so **France does / does France**.
Residents of this district pay more in local taxes than **city centre residents do / do city centre residents**.
The director was asked to stand aside, such **the pressure was / was the pressure** from the board.
So exhausted **she was / was she** after the marathon that she fell asleep on the train home.

sections.title: F: Cleft Sentences
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences, starting with the words given.

items.context: The laboratory window was smashed by a falling tree branch.
items.answer: It was a falling tree branch that smashed the laboratory window.

items.context: I will never forget the day I discovered I had been accepted to medical school.
items.sentence: The day on ......
items.answer: The day on which I discovered I had been accepted to medical school is one I'll never forget.

items.context: Nathan became a teacher to inspire students who had struggled at school.
items.sentence: The reason ......
items.answer: The reason Nathan became a teacher was to inspire students who had struggled at school.

items.context: Completing the entire marathon without stopping was the only thing the runner wanted.
items.answer: All the runner wanted was to complete the entire marathon without stopping.

items.context: The cyclist failed to notice that the traffic light had turned red.
items.sentence: What happened ......
items.answer: What happened was that the cyclist failed to notice the traffic light had turned red.

items.sentence: The person ......
items.answer: The person who discovered polonium was Marie Curie.

items.context: Watching my elderly neighbour struggle alone made me feel deeply ashamed.
items.sentence: What ......
items.answer: What made me feel deeply ashamed was watching my elderly neighbour struggle alone.

items.context: What we had to do first was reach agreement on a realistic budget.
items.sentence: The first thing ......
items.answer: The first thing we had to do was reach agreement on a realistic budget.

items.context: There are excellent walking trails where we usually go on holiday.
items.sentence: The place ......
items.answer: The place where we usually go on holiday has excellent walking trails.

items.context: Darwin demonstrated that species change gradually over many generations.
items.sentence: What ......
items.answer: What Darwin demonstrated was that species change gradually over many generations.

sections.title: G: so, such, too, enough
sections.instructions: Write so, such, too or enough in each gap.

items.sentence: It was ………………… impressive a speech that the assembled delegates gave her a standing ovation.
It's becoming increasingly obvious that there simply aren't ………………… trained volunteers to run the programme effectively.
I'm really enjoying my law degree, but I just find contract law ………………… dry and repetitive!
The children seemed to be having ………………… a wonderful time at the theme park that we ended up staying an extra hour.
The whole team had been pushing ………………… hard to meet the deadline that the manager treated everyone to a team lunch.
I worry about my parents because they still have ………………… a large mortgage to pay off.
It's ………………… early to say with any confidence whether the new treatment will prove effective.
Make sure you set off early ………………… to get a decent seat at the front.
I had no idea the funding proposal had ………………… few supporters on the committee.
Everyone was impressed when Lena passed with distinction because it is ………………… a rigorous and demanding examination.
Six months after the flood, the family had ………………… many urgent repairs to deal with that they felt completely overwhelmed.
I was hoping to catch James before he left the office, but I'm afraid it seems ………………… late now.

sections.title: H: Sentence Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given in bold. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
I had to organise all the documentation before I could submit the application.
**ORGANISED** Only when I ...... submit the application.
items.answer: had organised all the documentation could I

items.sentence:
The server crashed again as soon as we got it back online.
**HAD** Hardly ...... it crashed again.
items.answer: had we got the server back online when

items.sentence:
The family moved to a new city and very soon after they began to regret it.
**HAD** No sooner ...... they began to regret it.
items.answer: had the family moved to a new city than

items.sentence:
I had no idea my line manager was planning to offer me a promotion.
**REALISE** Little ...... my line manager was planning to offer me a promotion.

A colleague told me about the new funding opportunity.
**WHO** It was ...... me about the new funding opportunity.

Finding decent short-notice accommodation in the city centre isn't often possible.
**TO** Rarely ...... decent short-notice accommodation in the city centre.

They didn't ask me a single thing about my research background.
**QUESTION** Not one question ...... about my research background.

There is no situation in which we will allow the management to reduce the standard of care.
**NO** Under no circumstances ...... the standard of care.
items.answer: will we allow the management to reduce

items.sentence: Not until the full safety inspection is complete ………………… allowed to re-enter the building.
It was ……………………… a performance that even the most demanding critics had to applaud.
At no time ……………………… that the plan would fail so spectacularly.
So ……………………… that they actually finished the project two months ahead of schedule.
Never ……………………… complete and utter nonsense in all my years of reading!
In no way ……………………… that the council intends to reduce funding for arts education.
If you don't read the assembly instructions …………………, you might put the equipment together incorrectly.
No sooner ……………………… the rumour began to spread throughout the whole organisation.
options: A had the news broken than
B did the news break when
D the news had broken than

items.sentence: 'I can't believe how expensive everything in this city has become!' 'No, ……………………… . It's quite shocking, isn't it?'
'We visited the science museum last Saturday.' 'Did you? So ……………………… !'

sections.title: J: Cloze Text
sections.instructions: Complete the text with one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Seldom (1) ................. flatmates manage to share a home for years without having at least one serious disagreement.
Of course, many shared households function well, but only if there is a genuine willingness to compromise (2) .................... this achievable.
(3) ................... widespread are tensions in shared accommodation that dedicated mediation services now exist to resolve disputes over cleaning, noise and shared expenses.
Before things go (4) .................... far for the friendship to be repaired, there are several practical steps worth taking.
The first is (5) .................... sit down with your flatmate and have a calm, honest conversation.
You'd be amazed at how many conflicts are resolved simply by taking (6) .................... a straightforward step.
Not (7) .................... does it give you the chance to explain how you feel, it (8) .................... allows the other person to share their own perspective and, perhaps, take action.
On (9) .................... account should you remove or throw away a flatmate's belongings, however frustrated you may be feeling.
(10) .................. until you are absolutely certain you are acting within your legal rights should you consider taking any formal action.
